coredata.dc:title: Promising energy transfer system between fuorine and nitrogen Co-doped graphene quantum dots and Rhodamine B for ratiometric and visual detection of doxycycline in food
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Siyuan
Chen, Da
Li, Xiameng
Wang, Changxing
Li, Tingting
Ma, Jiaxing
Guo, Guoqiang
Guo, Qinglei
coredata.dc:description: A novel sensor based on dual emissive fluorescent graphene quantum dots is developed for a rapid, selective, sensitive and visual detection of doxycycline (DOX). The ratiometric fluorescent probe is designed by grafting fluorescent group (Rhodamine B, RhB) on F, N-doped graphene quantum dots (FNGQDs). In the presence of DOX, the fluorescence at 466 nm is remarkably quenched due to inner filter effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer, whereas the peak at 592 nm is attenuated slightly due to the energy transfer in the emission peaks of FNGQDs and RhB functional group. The sensor shows good linear relationship from 0.04 to 100 µM with a low detection limit of 40 nM. Furthermore, the flexible solid-state fluorescent sensing platform is used for detecting DOX in milk, pork and water samples. Therefore, this dual-emission FGQD-RhB can be used as a high-performance fluorescent and visual sensor for food safety and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual emissive fluorescence
Doxycycline detection
Ratiometric sensor
Visual quantitation
Flexible solid-state fluorescent sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of polyvinyl alcohol and FNGQDs-RhB composite film
Optical properties of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB
Selectivity and interference of FNGQDs-RhB for DOX
Visual detection of DOX by FNGQDs-RhB fluorescent film
Determination of DOX in real samples

This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204, 61604084, and 51802337). K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the preparation process of dual-emission FNGQDs-RhB and DOX detection in real sample. (b) XPS spectra of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB. The magnified C 1 s spectra of (c) FNGQDs and (d) FNGQDs-RhB. (e) FT-IR spectra of FNGQDs (black line) and FNGQDs-RhB (red line). TEM images of (f) FNGQDs and (g) FNGQDs-RhB (Inset shows the HR-TEM images and particle size distribution measured by TEM).

UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra of (a) FNGQDs and (d) FNGQDs-RhB (Inset: the optical images of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right)). Emission spectra of (b) FNGQDs at different excitation wavelengths (300–430 nm) and (e) FNGQDs-RhB at different excitation wavelengths (300–480 nm). Excitation-emission matrix graph maps for (c) FNGQDs and (f) FNGQDs-RhB.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of FNGQDs-RhB with gradually increased concentration of DOX. (b) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F466/F592) and the concentration of DOX. Inset: linear fitting between F466/F592 and the concentration of DOX within a wide range from 0 to 100 μM. (c) Images of FNGQDs-RhB in the presence of various concentrations of DOX under the excitation of daylight and UV light. (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum of (i) DOX solution, (ii) FNGQDs-RhB solution, (iii) FNGQDs-RhB@DOX compound aqueous solution and (iv) theoretical curve of curve i + curve ii. (e) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of DOX, and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the FNGQDs-RhB. (f) The fluorescence decay profile of the FNGQDs-RhB in the absence and presence of DOX.
Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching mechanism inside of FNGQDs-RhB.

The images of FNGQDs-RhB fluorescence film (a) with DOX at different concentration, (b) in the presence of different tetracycline drugs, (c) in real samples. The images of FNGQDs-RhB fluorescence film under 365 nm UV light (I) and the film image is transferred into the 3D model by Image J software (II).
Schematic diagram of DOX residue detection in real sample by FNGQDs-RhB/PVA fluorescence film.
Determination of DOX in real samples using fluorescence and HPLC techniques.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *The critical t-value at the 95% confidence level is 2.78.

head.ce:title: Promising energy transfer system between fuorine and nitrogen Co-doped graphene quantum dots and Rhodamine B for ratiometric and visual detection of doxycycline in food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Microelectronics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Microelectronics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel sensor based on dual emissive fluorescent graphene quantum dots is developed for a rapid, selective, sensitive and visual detection of doxycycline (DOX). The ratiometric fluorescent probe is designed by grafting fluorescent group (Rhodamine B, RhB) on F, N-doped graphene quantum dots (FNGQDs). In the presence of DOX, the fluorescence at 466 nm is remarkably quenched due to inner filter effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer, whereas the peak at 592 nm is attenuated slightly due to the energy transfer in the emission peaks of FNGQDs and RhB functional group. The sensor shows good linear relationship from 0.04 to 100 µM with a low detection limit of 40 nM. Furthermore, the flexible solid-state fluorescent sensing platform is used for detecting DOX in milk, pork and water samples. Therefore, this dual-emission FGQD-RhB can be used as a high-performance fluorescent and visual sensor for food safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the invention of antibiotics, diseases related to bacterial infections have been controlled effectively. Among them, tetracyclines (TCs) antibiotics, as a kind of multifunctional broad-spectrum antibiotics, are widely used to heal bacterial infections in animals and humans because they can inhibit the protein synthesis of bacteria (Xing, Huang, Zhao, Xiao, & Lan, 2020). Unfortunately, excessive abuse of antibiotics has had a series of extremely serious consequences (Lan, Yao, Ping, & Ying, 2017; Yao, Jiang, & Ping, 2019). The residual TCs may lead to selective genetic variation of microorganisms, leading to the emergence of drug-resistant pathogens. Bacteria can also obtain antibiotic resistance genes through gene mutation or gene transfer (Yan, Sun, Pang, Jiang, & Zheng, 2020). At the same time, the worldwide abuse of antibiotics leads to the widespread spread and transfer of resistant microorganisms between humans and animals, which greatly endangers the health and safety of human beings (Xing, Huang, Zhao, Xiao, & Lan, 2020). Therefore, the detection of tetracycline antibiotics residues is very important for human health and food safety.

Doxycycline (DOX) is a semisynthetic tetracyclines antibiotic obtained by the modification of oxytetracycline to treat diseases with excellent efficacy in the modern medical field. It has excellent resistance to infection against both gram-positive bacteria and negative bacteria, such as rickettsia, mycoplasma, large viruses, spirochetes, chlamydia and some protozoa (Newton, et al., 2005; Palamy & Ruengsitagoon, 2017). Meanwhile, DOX also has the advantages of low cost and good oral absorption, so it has been widely used in animal husbandry (Tian & Fan, 2021). However, the presence of DOX in the environment can also cause antibiotic resistance and seriously hazard to human and animal health. In addition, excessive intake of DOX will cause human liver damage and kidney harm, as well as affect the growth and formation of teeth (Siddiqui, et al., 2020). To ensure the health and safety of the public, the European Union has established maximum limit for DOX antibiotic residues in foods, e.g., 225 nM (100 ng/mL) in milk or meat (Yu, et al., 2020). Therefore, the analytical method for DOX detection with simplicity, rapidness and accuracy is necessary to be settled.

Currently, the primary methods for DOX detection still rely on laboratorial instrumental analysis including liquid/gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), surface enhanced Raman scattering, enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), electrochemical analysis and capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Tian & Fan, 2021; Xing, Huang, Zhao, Xiao, & Lan, 2020; Yu, et al., 2020). Nevertheless, these methods cannot meet the requirements for rapid detection of trace DOX due to the disadvantages of expensive instruments, complex operation, tedious pretreatments and time-consuming analysis. In contrast, due to the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity and simplicity, fluorescence analysis method has been widely used for DOX detection (Sun, et al., 2021; Yu, et al., 2020). Recently, fluorometric graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterial, have received much attention from scientists because of their superiorities involving facile synthesis, splendid water solubility, high sensitivity and chemical stability (Boonta, et al., 2020; Li, Guo, Pei, & Lin, 2020). However, most of the fluorescence analyses are based on single signal changes, such as fluorescence quenching or enhancement (Hu, Gao, & Luo, 2021; Pu, et al., 2021). These fluorescence analyses are susceptible to interference from precision and stability of instrument, background noises and the concentration of fluorescence probe (Yao, et al., 2021). In order to solve these problems, the development of GQDs with ratiometric fluorescent sensing capability is highly desirable. Due to the presence of internal standards, ratiometric methods have a built-in correction for environmental disturbance effects so that the fluorescence intensities of ratiometric nanoprobe are not affected by uncontrollable experimental conditions, such as the matrix background and instrument efficiency (Yao, et al., 2021). At present, most ratiometric fluorescence probes for DOX detection are obtained by embedding or linking fluorescent material to the non-fluorescent or fluorescent matrix (Fu, et al., 2018; Li, et al., 2021; Tian & Fan, 2021). For example, Li et al. prepared ratiometric fluorescent probes based on a polynuclear lanthanide metal–organic framework for the detection of DOX (Li, et al., 2021). Tian et al. designed a ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor by the rare earth-carbon dots nanocomposite for the determination of DOX (Tian & Fan, 2021). Fu et al. integrated ethanediamine-modified carbon dots with luminescent metal organic frameworks (MOF) to form a ratiometric fluorescence nanoplatform for the detection of DOX (Fu, et al., 2018). However, these methods are accompanied by complex processes, high costs and uncontrollable synthesis. In addition, with increasing requirements in terms of high sensitivity and accurate real-time detection, film detection has attracted more attentions as potential sample-to-answer analyzers because they are portable and easy to operate (Hu, et al., 2021). Hence, it is very necessary to construct a feasible route of ratiometric fluorescent probes with dual emission for fluorescent and visual detection.

Rhodamine B (RhB), as an organic fluorescent molecule, is widely used as a ligand of various complexes. In this work, we focus on the development of a ratiometric optical dual-mode probe by grafting fluorescent group (RhB) on FNGQDs for the detection of DOX. FNGQDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal carbonization of levofloxacin in deionized water. The as-synthesized FNGQDs-RhB exhibit dual photoluminescence emissions centered at 466 nm and 592 nm. Upon the addition of DOX, the fluorescence at 466 nm is remarkably quenched based on promising energy transfer system between FNGQDs and RhB (inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)), and the emission center at 592 nm is slightly attenuated by the energy transfer in the emission peaks of FNGQDs and RhB functional group. As such, a ratiometric sensor for DOX detection is developed based on FNGQDs-RhB and shows an excellent linear regression in the concentration range from 0.04 to 100 μM of DOX by calculating fluorescent intensity ratio, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 40 nM. Simultaneously, a novel kind of FNGQDs-RhB film is also developed for rapid and visual DOX detection in real samples including milk, tap water and other samples. This work will contribute to the specific design of functional GQDs and broaden the portable detection methods for analytical detection in food.

Levofloxacin (98%), Rhodamine B (98%), ethanol, doxycycline (DOX), tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), methacycline (MTC), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), NaOH, H2SO4, CuCl2, CaCl2, CoCl2, ZnCl2, AlCl3, MgSO4, NaCl, SnCl2, CdCl2, BaCl2, K2CO3, HgCl2, AgCl, PbCl2 and other chemicals were provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China) and were used as received without further purification. The required ultrapure water was prepared through a Millipore water purification system.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR-iS5 spectrophotometer in the 400–4000 cm−1 range with samples prepared as KBr pellets. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin (FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA) microscope. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed by using Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Escalab 250Xi, USA) was employed to determine the surface chemical composition and functional groups. UV spectra were recorded on a UV-5900 PC spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were obtained from HITACHI F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer at room temperature. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were taken on the FL3-111 spectrometer (Horiba, France) at room temperature. The excitation and emission wavelengths were set at 388 and 466 nm, respectively. All fluorescence photographs were taken with iPhone 12 under AGL-9406 portable ultraviolet lamp (365 nm). The high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis experiment was carried out using a HPLC system (Shimadzu, LC-20AT series) with the UV detector (268 nm).

FNGQDs-RhB were prepared via a typical hydrothermal method (He, Zhuang, Yu, Li, & Liu, 2021; Pu, et al., 2021). 2 mmol (0.72 g) levofloxacin was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 6 hrs. After cooling down to room temperature, the product solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 15 min) (Jia, et al., 2021). The supernatant was filtered utilizing a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and the fluorescent FNGQDs were obtained after dialysis against distilled water for 24 hrs in a dialysis kit (1000 Da MWCO) (Zhao, et al., 2021). Then, 2 mmol (0.96 g) RhB were added to the purified FNGQDs solution and the mixed solution was transferred to the autoclave. The reaction was carried out at 140 °C for 8 hrs. Subsequently, the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove insoluble large particles and the filtrate was freeze-dried to obtain the FNGQDs-RhB powder.

Quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) and Rhodamine B (QY = 0.56) was chosen as a standard. The quantum yields of FNGQDs-RhB were calculated according to the following Equation (Zhao, et al., 2021),
(1)
φ =
φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2
Where φ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, A refers to the absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength, and the subscript R refers to the reference standard with a known quantum yield.

The FNGQDs-RhB water solution (0.5 mg·mL−1) was added to a series of solution containing different concentrations of DOX from 0 to 600 μM. After the solution mixed uniformly, the emission spectra of these mixtures were recorded at the optimal excitation wavelength (388 nm).

In order to evaluate the selectivity and interference of mental ions and other tetracyclines antibiotics to FNGQDs-RhB during the detection of DOX, the fluorescence intensity of FNGQDs-RhB without DOX was recorded under 100 μM and 600 μM ions and TCs, including Cu2+, Co2+, Ag+, Hg2+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Na+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, TC, CTC, OTC and MTC. Meanwhile, a series of interfering ions (100 μM) were added into FNGQDs-RhB solution with 100 μM DOX. And the fluorescence intensity was recorded under the optimal excitation and emission wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of polyvinyl alcohol and FNGQDs-RhB composite film
ce:section.ce:para: FNGQDs-RhB fluorescent composite films were fabricated by mixing FNGQDs-RhB into a non-luminescent polymer (PVA). 0.05 g PVA and 0.01 g FNGQDs-RhB powder were mixed in 20 mL water and stirred at 70 °C for 30 min. To ensure good dispersion, FNGQDs-RhB were dispersed by bath sonication for 10 min. Subsequently, 5 mL of FNGQDs-RhB and PVA mixture was taken in a petri dish and dried for 24 hrs at room temperature.

For real sample analysis, milk, pork, tap water, human blood plasma, waste water and Yongjiang river water were selected. The milk, tap water and pork were bought from the local market near Ningbo University. The human blood plasma was obtained from a volunteer at Ningbo University. Waste water was collected from the campus of Ningbo University. The 100 mL liquid samples (milk, waste water and Yongjiang river water) were filtered by 0.22 μm Millipore filter to remove the solid impurity. 5 mL of plasma sample and 5 mL of 10% (w/v) trichloroacetic acid were mixed in a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min to remove the proteins, then the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter to eliminate particulate matters (Zhuang, et al., 2021). Subsequently, the liquid samples after treatment were spiked with the different levels of DOX and well mixed for later detection. As for pork, 5 g was weighted into 50 mL centrifuge tube and then spiked with proper level of DOX. Next, 10 mL of water was added into the tube and mixed it well. Finally, after the sample centrifuged, 5 mL of the leachate was transferred after centrifuging for 10 min at 5000 rpm for later detection (Xing, Huang, Zhao, Xiao, & Lan, 2020). HPLC was used as a reference method, and the corresponding result of t-test was analyzed (Dihuidi, Kucharski, Roets, Hoogmartens, & Vanderhaeghe, 1985).

Based on the FNGQDs synthesized by levofloxacin, RhB is grafted to obtain dual emission FNGQDs-RhB, as shown in Fig. 1
(a). The functional groups and terminal elements of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB are studied by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1 (b), the full XPS spectra exhibit four peaks at 285.1 eV, 399.9 eV, 532.2 eV and 686.5 eV, which are assigned to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and F 1 s, respectively. The corresponding detailed atomic concentrations are shown in Table S1. The possible chemical species in each major element are further determined via the deconvolution of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and F 1 s peaks. As illustrated in Fig. 1 (c), the C 1 s peak of FNGQDs is deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.38 eV, 285.38 eV, 286.18 eV and 288.88 eV, which correspond to sp2 carbon (CC), sp3 carbon (CO/CN), C-F and carbonyl groups (HO-CO), respectively (Wang, et al., 2021). However, a new peak of NH-CO at 290.1 eV is observed in FNGQDs-RhB (Fig. 1 (d)), which can be assigned to amide groups, presumably due to dehydration condensation between the carboxylic group of RhB and amino group of FNGQDs (Li, et al., 2018). Meanwhile, there are much more CC groups in FNGQDs-RhB (Table S2), which indicates the synthesized FNGQDs-RhB have higher degree of graphitization and extensive conjugated sp2 domains (Zhao, et al., 2021). The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB exhibit three peaks for pyridinic N (399.18 eV), amino N (400.48 eV) and graphitic N (401.78 eV), respectively ( Figure S1
(a-b)) (Yan, et al., 2019). Comparing FNGQDs with FNGQDs-RhB detailed element ratio (Table S3), the amino content of FNGQDs-RhB decreased, indicating that the amino group on FNGQDs successfully reacts with the carboxyl group of RhB. The O 1 s spectra of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB show peaks at 530.98 eV, 532.9 eV and 535.2 eV, which correspond to CO, CO and COC, respectively ( Figure S1
(c-d)) (Jayaweera, Yin, & Ng, 2019; Wang, et al., 2020). Notably, the content of CO groups on FNGQDs-RhB increase from 48.10% to 52.43% compared to FNGQDs (Table S4), suggesting that more CO are formed on FNGQDs-RhB by grafting. In the F 1 s spectra of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB ( Figure S1
(e-f)), the peaks ascribed to semi-ionic C-F and covalent C-F are found at 684.38 eV and 686.88 eV (Long, et al., 2018).

The functional groups on the surface of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB are characterized by FT-IR technique (Fig. 1 (e)). For FNGQDs, the stretching vibration peaks of –OH/NH, CO, C-F and COC are located at 3451 cm−1, 1734 cm−1, 1297 cm−1 and 995 cm−1, respectively (Hu, et al., 2021; Long, et al., 2018). The absorption peak between 2801 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of CH bond (Hu, et al., 2020). For FNGQDs-RhB, these peaks are also observed, which indicate that FNGQDs-RhB inherit the natural properties of FNGQDs during the synthesis process. In addition, sharp peaks associated with amide linkage (–CONH-) at 1734 cm−1 and 1702 cm−1 prove the formation of abundant amide bonds and oxygen-containing functional group in FNGQDs-RhB, which is consistent with XPS results (He, Zhuang, Yu, Li, & Liu, 2021). Comparing these spectra of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB, the results indicate that -RhB groups are grafted onto the FNGQDs surfaces via amide bond.

Figure 1 (f) and (g) show the TEM images of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB. These spherical FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB are uniformly dispersed. The HR-TEM images show lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.24 nm that agrees well with the (100) in-plane spacing of graphene (Liu, et al., 2020). The average diameters of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB are 3.27 nm and 4.52 nm, respectively. Similarly, we also study the dynamic light scattering (DLS) for estimation of hydrodynamic diameter of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB ( Figure S2 ). It is found that the average hydrodynamic diameters of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB are 3.45 nm and 4.82 nm, which is in good accordance with TEM data (Fig. 1 (f-g)). It indicates that the diameter of the synthesized FNGQDs-RhB increased after the grafting of FNGQDs with RhB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of FNGQDs and FNGQDs-RhB are evaluated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy at room temperature. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of FNGQDs is recorded in Fig. 2
(a). The three absorption peaks at 223 nm, 249 nm and 282 nm are derived from π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains (CC), while the other two peaks around 332 nm and 348 nm are ascribed to the n-π* transition of the heteroatomic bond (CO/CN) (Liu, et al., 2017). In addition, FNGQDs exhibit a broad absorption from 378 nm to 418 nm, which is due to the effect of surface states or the defect bonds (CN/C-F) (Long, et al., 2018). The fluorescence spectrum demonstrates that the emission of FNGQDs occurs at 466 nm under optimal excitation wavelength (388 nm), and the FNGQDs solution is brown under the sunlight and exhibits strong blue fluorescence under irradiation with UV light. Moreover, the FNGQDs aqueous solution exhibits an excitation-dependent fluorescence feature (Fig. 2 (b) and Fig. 2 (c)). By comparison, the peaks near the center at 554 nm in the UV absorbance spectrum of FNGQDs-RhB (Fig. 2 (d)) are ascribed to -RhB absorption while retaining the absorption peak positions of the original FNGQDs (Guo, Song, Cai, & Wang, 2020). And the FNGQDs-RhB exhibit two fluorescence peaks under single excitation (388 nm): the emission peaks at 466 nm and 592 nm. The photographs of FNGQDs-RhB dispersions in the inset of Fig. 2 (d) show the strong red color under daylight and pink fluorescence light under 365 nm UV light. Comparing excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behaviors of emission peak of FNGQDs, the emission peak at 592 nm of -RhB barely changes (Fig. 2 (e)). Fig. 2 (f) exhibits the excitation-emission matrix of the FNGQDs-RhB and two different emission centers (466 nm and 592 nm) are reflected. Interestingly, compared with the two-dimensional mapping spectrum of RhB ( Figure S3 ), the FNGQDs-RhB exhibit stronger red emission in the excitation range of 390–430 nm, which is attributed to the possible energy transfer between the FNGQDs luminescence center and the -RhB functional group (Wang, Peng, Luo, Wu, & Cui, 2021). To verify this, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of RhB are measured. As shown in Figure S4 , a significant spectral overlap is found between the fluorescence of the energy donor (FNGQDs) and the absorption of the acceptor (-RhB), ensuring the favorable energy transfer and enhancing the fluorescence intensity of the compact red emission region (Wang, Peng, Luo, Wu, & Cui, 2021). The quantum yields (QYs) of the emission peaks at 466 nm and 592 nm are calculated to be 61% and 43%, respectively.

For practical applications in fluorescence-based probes, the fluorescent stabilities of FNGQDs-RhB under different conditions are required. Figure S5
(a) exhibits the fluorescence intensity of FNGQDs-RhB at 466 nm and 592 nm at different pH values, which remained at 93% at a wide pH range from 2 to 12. As shown in Figure S5
(b) and Figure S5
(c), the fluorescence intensity maintains 90% after the UV exposure (365 nm) for 12 hrs, and keeps 92% after storing at room temperature up to 20 days. The above analysis results demonstrate that the synthesized FNGQDs-RhB possess good optical performance and outstanding stability, suggesting the immense potential in various practical situations.

The sensing property of FNGQDs-RhB is evaluated by the detection of DOX under the excitation of 388 nm. Figure S6 displays that the quenching reaction reached a complete state in the 180 s after the addition of DOX. Therefore, 180 s is chosen as the optimal response time for further exploring the performance of the FNGQDs-RhB-based fluorescence sensor system. Fig. 3
(a) exhibits the change in fluorescence intensity as a function of DOX concentration ranging from 0 to 600 μM. With the increase of the DOX concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 466 nm gradually decreases, whereas the emission center at 592 nm is slightly weakened. Based on such unique property of FNGQDs-RhB, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for DOX detection is established. By calculating the emission intensity ratio of F466/F592 versus the concentration of DOX in the range from 0 to 600 μM (F466 and F592 are the fluorescence intensities of FNGQDs-RhB at 466 nm and 592 nm, respectively), a good linear relationship is obtained in the range of 0.04–100 μM with R2 = 0.998 (Fig. 3 (b), the relevant parameters for the regression curve are shown in Tables S5-S7), which is better than that obtained from the single-channel calculation of fluorescence intensity change at 466 nm (R2 = 0.996, Figure S7 ). The LOD is estimated to be 40 nM, which is far lower than the maximum level of waste water discharge (0.5 mg/L) permitted by the European Union Standard (European Food Safety Authority, & European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control, 2019). Meanwhile, the pink color of FNGQDs-RhB solution was slightly lighter due to the dyes properties of the fluorescent group (-RhB), whereas the fluorescence color of FNGQDs-RhB changes regularly with the increase of DOX under UV lamp (pink to red), as shown in Fig. 3 (c). The distinctive color variations make this ratiometric sensing system simpler and more convenient for the detection of DOX compared with those single wavelength intensity modulation type sensors.

Subsequently, the sensing mechanism of FNGQDs-RhB to DOX is discussed. Fig. 3 (d) exhibits the UV–vis absorbance of FNGQDs-RhB (curve i), DOX (curve ii), FNGQDs-RhB@DOX compound aqueous solution (curve iii) and the sum of curve i + curve ii (curve iv). The observed ignorable peaks shift reveals that no complex is formed between FNGQDs-RhB to DOX. The FT-IR spectra of FNGQDs-RhB in the presence and absence of DOX in Figure S8 show that no obvious shift and no new absorption site appear, indicating that no new functional groups are formed or adhered at the surface of FNGQDs-RhB (Zhou, He, Ya, Zhong, & Deng, 2017). In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DOX and fluorescence spectrum of FNGQDs-RhB are recorded in Fig. 3 (e). The broad absorption peak at 360 nm of DOX has a large overlap with the excitation peak and a small overlap with the emission peak of FNGQDs-RhB. This means that the fluorescence quenching phenomenon may be caused by the synergistic effect of internal filtering effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Supchocksoonthorn, et al., 2021). Generally, for FRET, the quencher shortens the fluorescence lifetime of the donor, while for IFE, it does not. As shown in Fig. 3 (f), the average fluorescence lifetimes of FNGQDs-RhB in the absence and presence of DOX are 6.81 and 6.42 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime changes slightly, suggesting that the IFE dominates the fluorescence quenching and FRET serves as a subordinated factor.

The intrinsic fluorescence quenching mechanism of FNGQDs-RhB is shown in Fig. 4 . The detection mechanism of DOX includes two parts: the competitive energy absorption between FNGQDs-RhB and DOX (Fig. 4 (a)) and the transfer of emission energy from FNGQDs-RhB and DOX (Fig. 4 (b)). The IFE is confirmed by the overlap between the excitation spectrum of FNGQDs-RhB and DOX absorption between 285 and 400 nm (blue area in Fig. 3 (e)), resulting in a less excitation to FNGQDs-RhB (Sun, et al., 2021). As shown in Figure S9 , the band gaps of FNGQDs-RhB and DOX are determined to be 2.47 and 2.36 eV, respectively. The similar and slightly larger optical band gap of FNGQDs-RhB than that of DOX suggests that the competitive energy absorption is plausible. The FRET is also confirmed by the overlap between FNGQDs-RhB emission and DOX absorption between 350 and 460 nm (orange area in Fig. 3 (e)). This led to a reduced fluorescence emission of the FNGQDs-RhB. The synergistic effect of less excitation and energy transfer thus led to a fluorescence quenching of FNGQDs-RhB in the presence of DOX. Meanwhile, slight attenuation of the emission peak at 592 nm is also observed with increasing DOX concentration. This is attributed to the reduced energy transfer between the emission peak at 466 nm and 592 nm (Fig. 4 (c)). From these results, we can conclude that the favorable IFE and FRET led to a significant reduction in fluorescence emission of the FNGQDs-RhB in the presence of DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of FNGQDs-RhB for DOX
ce:section.ce:para:
The selectivity of FNGQDs-RhB towards DOX is investigated. As shown in Figure S10
(a), four analogues to DOX (TC, CTC, OTC and MTC) can obviously quench the fluorescence of the FNGQDs-RhB, and other interfering substances (Cu2+, Co2+, Ag+, Hg2+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Na+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Pb2+ and Sn2+) cause only tiny fluorescence variation even when the concentration of these ions is up to 600 μM. The results clearly demonstrate that the FNGQDs-RhB have good specificity for the determination of tetracyclines antibiotics and are expected to be an efficient fluorescent sensor for TCs. In this study, we choose DOX as model molecules for detection TCs. In addition, the above metals ions are commonly found in agricultural products, such as fruits, vegetables, water and nutrient solutions, thus it is necessary to monitor the anti-interference capability of FNGQDs-RhB during detection (Wang, et al., 2020). Figure S10
(b) exhibits that the presence of the interfering substances has no significant effect on the determination of DOX. In general, all results strongly demonstrate that the FNGQDs-RhB based ratiometric and colorimetric probe has high selectivity and strong anti-interference ability for DOX detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of DOX by FNGQDs-RhB fluorescent film
ce:section.ce:para:
Base on the visualization of FNGQDs-RhB fluorescence color changes, a quick response, simple, high selectiveness and portable DOX detection fluorescent film is developed. As shown in Fig. 5
(a), FNGQDs-RhB/PVA film exhibits a pink color under the UV light (365 nm). When the different concentrations of DOX are added, the fluorescence intensities of FNGQDs-RhB/PVA film are quenched immediately, and the color changes could be observed by naked eyes under 365 nm UV light. Figure S11 exhibits the time response curve of FNGQDS-RhB/PVA film to DOX, which reach a complete state in the 240 s after the addition of DOX. As shown in Fig. 5 (b), 100 μM of other tetracycline drugs, for example tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and methacycline (MTC), are used to contrast with DOX signal on the fluorescence film. Under the 365 nm ultraviolet light, the FNGQDs-RhB fluorescence films represent a similar quenching effect on tetracycline drugs. Subsequently, we test the actual samples added with 100 μM DOX by the above films, and find that the fluorescence color and intensity of the films exhibit significant changes (Fig. 5 (c)). These results indicate that the composite film has the potential as a rapid and visual tool for DOX detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DOX in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its wide range of medical uses, DOX is readily enriched in animals and food products, and then accumulates in the food chain, thus posing potential threats due to the toxic properties of DOX.(Sun, et al., 2021) Therefore, 100 μM of DOX is spiked to the meat leachate for detection (Fig. 6 ). The results show that it is feasible to detect DOX residues in food by FNGQDs-RhB/PVA film. Therefore, a portable detection device based on FNGQDs-RhB/PVA film is a potential and excellent detection method for DOX in food.

In addition, we also measure DOX in milk, tap water, human blood plasma, waste water and Yongjiang river samples to validate the practicability of the FNGQDs-RhB. In order to evaluate the feasibility of the ratiometric fluorescence method for detecting DOX in samples, HPLC is used as a reference method, and the corresponding result of t-test is analyzed (Dihuidi, Kucharski, Roets, Hoogmartens, & Vanderhaeghe, 1985; Guo, et al., 2021). For both HPLC and fluorescence methods, the percentage recoveries are in the acceptable range of 94.3–110.7% and the relative standard deviation in percentage (%RSD) is lower than 4.55, indicating that our proposed method has excellent accuracy and reliability (Table 1 ). Moreover, the t-test shows no significant differences between both methods at a 95% confidence level. These results of real samples verify that the FNGQDs-RhB have good practicability for monitoring DOX in practical systems. Finally, compared with the previous literature reports listed in Table S8, we find that all these chemosensors are capable of detecting DOX; however, they still suffer from a tedious synthesis procedure. The FNGQDs-RhB presented in this work has good performance in terms of the LOD and detection concentration range without the use of tedious methods, which has good application potential in the on-site detection of the DOX residue in food.

In summary, we proposed an effective strategy for synthesizing FNGQDs-RhB by grafting fluorescent group (RhB) on FNGQDs, which can serve as a high-performance fluorescent and visual sensor for DOX. The prepared FNGQDs-RhB exhibit dual emission behaviors, which originate from different luminescence centers. The blue emission is attributed to the FNGQDs, and the red emission at 592 nm is closely related to the grafted -RhB functional group. The emission centered at 466 nm of FNGQDs-RhB is remarkably quenched based on the IFE and FRET, and the emission centered at 592 nm is slight attenuation due to the reduced energy transfer between the emission peak at 466 nm and 592 nm. As a result, a ratiometric fluorescent probe for DOX is developed based on the FNGQDs-RhB. With the increase concentration of DOX, the fluorescence intensity shows a linear relationship in the range of 0.04–100 μM by ratiometric sensing with the detection limit of 40 nM. Meanwhile, inspired by the rational design and application of portable fluorescence detection technology, a fluorescent film is developed for detection of DOX. The film exhibits excellent selectivity and agility to DOX detection. In addition, the method is used for visual quantitative detection of DOX. Thus, our approach provides a simple strategy for the preparation of dual-emission GQDs, which can be applied as viable fluorescent sensors to detect DOX residues at trace-level for food safety and environment monitoring.
Uncited References
Sun et al. (2021), Wang et al. (2020).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204, 61604084, and 51802337). K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.132936.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
